summary: NaH balanced coupled FCI with W1c on/off (Phase C-W1c residual orthogonality wall test, May 2026)
verdict: W1c-residual wall CONFIRMED: NaH PES still monotonically descending under screened=True; cross-V_ne reduction 17.5x but binding NOT recovered

screened_off_bare.label: bare cross-V_ne (Sprint 7 baseline)
screened_off_bare.diagnosis: monotonically descending PES, R_min at edge (R=2.5)

screened_on_w1c.label: W1c FrozenCore-aware screening
screened_on_w1c.diagnosis: still monotonically descending PES, R_min at edge (R=2.5); BINDING NOT RECOVERED

actual_attraction_reduction: 17.5x in PES descent magnitude (FCI-resolved, broader test than diagnostic single-R)
structural_finding: The W1c closure REDUCES overattraction by ~17.5x but does NOT eliminate it.
The PES still descends monotonically toward small R under both bare and screened cross-V_ne.
This empirically confirms the W1c-residual orthogonality wall named in Phase C diagnostic memo.
The mechanism (H valence orbital ↔ [Ne] core orthogonality, Phillips-Kleinman-class) is operative
The wall sits BEYOND the Hartree screening of Z_eff(rho) that W1c implements.

comparison_to_lih_first_row.interpretation: First-row LiH binds (Track CD ships at 7% R_eq error). Second-row NaH does not bind under either bare or screened W1c. The architectural distinction is: first-row uses an explicit core block in the FCI sector (electrons resolve their own orthogonality), while second-row uses a frozen-core potential (orthogonality must be enforced externally and W1c does not yet do so).

implications: The next chemistry-direction sprint should attack the W1c-residual orthogonality wall directly.
Phillips-Kleinman-class projection on the H valence orbital against the [Ne] core (in cross-center geometry) is the mechanical fix.
This is independent of the rest of the multi-focal closures: W1a/W1b are nuclear-electronic, W2b is theoretical NCG, W1c is a no-op for first-row.
The W1c-residual orthogonality wall is the SINGLE remaining engineering closure for second-row chemistry.
